unified_narrative.one_liner: A working demonstration of an AI treasury agent that reasons about cash positioning, FX exposure, liquidity forecasting, and funding decisions across entities, then executes payments deterministically through protocol-compliant infrastructure.
unified_narrative.core_thesis: Treasury management is genuinely non-deterministic: if it were deterministic, it would have been automated decades ago. Full-time treasurers exist because cash positioning, FX hedging, liquidity forecasting, and funding decisions require judgment over noisy, probabilistic data across multiple entities and jurisdictions. The agent lives in the treasury reasoning layer. Payments are the deterministic last mile.
unified_narrative.anchor_scenario: A corporate treasurer's AI agent wakes up to: GBP receivables arriving next week (probabilistic), SGD payroll due Friday (fixed), a supplier in Lagos waiting on $10K (flexible timing), and cash scattered across entities in Singapore, London, and Delaware. The agent reasons about which entity should fund which obligation, whether to hedge the GBP/USD exposure or wait, which corridor and rail minimizes total cost for the Lagos payment, and whether to draw on the revolver or liquidate a short-term investment to cover the SGD shortfall.
unified_narrative.why_agents_not_microservices: The payment execution (send X to Y via Z) is deterministic and belongs in microservices. The treasury decision (what to fund, from where, when, how to manage FX exposure, which funding source) is non-deterministic because it depends on probabilistic cash flow forecasts, moving FX rates, competing entity needs, tax implications of intercompany flows, and policy constraints that interact in ways that resist static rules engines.

how_projects_connect.05-treasury-agent: The brain. The central agent that reasons about cash positioning across entities, FX exposure management, liquidity forecasting, and funding decisions. This is where the non-determinism lives and where an agent genuinely earns its inference cost. All other projects serve this agent.
how_projects_connect.01-mcp-payments-server: The deterministic execution layer. Exposes payment operations (initiate transfer, check status, get FX rates, sanctions screening) as MCP tools that the treasury agent calls after making its decisions. Proves that the payment itself is infrastructure, not intelligence.
how_projects_connect.02-adk-treasury-orchestrator: The multi-agent reasoning layer. A Google ADK system where the treasury agent delegates to specialist sub-agents: a cash positioning agent, an FX strategy agent, a compliance agent, and a funding agent. Each sub-agent handles one dimension of the treasury decision.
how_projects_connect.03-ap2-implementation: The trust layer. When the treasury agent decides to execute a payment, AP2/TAP/Verifiable Intent ensure the agent has cryptographic proof of authorization within specific constraints (amount limits, approved corridors, approved counterparties).
how_projects_connect.04-cross-border-routing: A capability within the treasury agent's toolkit. Once the agent decides which entity funds the Lagos payment, this module evaluates corridors and rails (SWIFT, stablecoins, mobile money, instant rails) to minimize cost for that specific leg. The rail selection has probabilistic elements (real-time FX spreads, fee variability across networks) but is narrower than the full treasury decision.
how_projects_connect.06-protocol-deep-dives: The thought leadership layer. Makes the protocol landscape legible to PMs and architects. Now framed around how each protocol serves treasury automation, not just payment routing.
how_projects_connect.07-payment-api-design: The engineering rigor. Production-grade API design for the deterministic layer: idempotency, webhook confirmations, error handling, reconciliation. Shows that the infrastructure beneath the agent is built to payment-grade standards.

build_philosophy.description: Working code that hits real APIs where possible (Alpha Vantage for FX, Plaid sandbox for accounts) but with mock payment backends and mock treasury data (multi-entity cash positions, forecasts). Polished enough for LinkedIn demos and GitHub engagement. Every demo shows both paths: the deterministic happy path (fast, cheap, boring) and the exception path where the agent actually reasons (FX decision, funding source selection, entity optimization).

projects.name: Cross-Border Routing Engine
projects.description: A corridor and rail optimization module that the treasury agent calls after deciding which entity funds a cross-border obligation. Evaluates real-time FX spreads, fee structures, and settlement speeds across SWIFT, stablecoins, mobile money, and instant rails. The rail selection has probabilistic elements (FX spread volatility, fee variability across networks, settlement time variance) but is narrower than the full treasury decision. This is a tool the treasury agent uses, not a standalone agent.

readme_spec.title: Cross-Border Routing Engine
readme_spec.subtitle: Corridor and rail optimization for cross-border payments. Called by the treasury agent after entity and funding decisions are made.
sections: Where this fits (treasury agent → funding decision → this module → payment execution)
Probabilistic elements (FX spread volatility, fee variability, settlement time variance)

root_readme_entry.demonstrates: Multi-rail corridor optimization (SWIFT, stablecoins, mobile money)

projects.name: Treasury Agent (Core)
projects.description: The central agent in the platform. Reasons about cash positioning across multi-jurisdictional entities, FX exposure management, liquidity forecasting over noisy receivables/payables data, and funding source selection (revolver draw, CP issuance, intercompany loan, investment liquidation). This is where the non-determinism lives: the decisions that keep full-time treasurers employed. Built with LangChain, OpenAI Functions, and MCP. Payments are downstream deterministic execution.

readme_spec.title: Treasury Agent
